477	\begin{lemma}\leanok
478	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
479	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
480	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
481	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
482	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars,
483	        lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt,
484	        lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
485	% NOTE: the carrier-wall obstruction to `hloc` is resolved (restriction-of-scalars /
486	% scalar-tower instances feed lem:powers_restrictScalars; lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule
487	% supplies the R'-side localization). The single remaining obligation is the open-naturality of
488	% the section comparison e_{D(a)}, established as a prose remark in the proof of
489	% lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt; the proof sketch below derives each per-a hloc(a) from the top-level
490	% localization plus that naturality square for the inclusion D(a) ⊆ ⊤, so no section-level square
491	% is hand-proved per a.
492	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
493	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
494	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
495	  \[
496	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
497	    \;\cong\;
498	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
499	  \]
500	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
501	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
502	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
503	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
504	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
505	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
506	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
507	
508	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
509	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
510	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
511	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
512	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
513	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
514	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
515	\end{lemma}
516	
517	\begin{proof}
518	  \leanok
519	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars, lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
520	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
521	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
522	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
523	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
524	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
525	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
526	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
527	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
528	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
529	
530	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
531	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
532	  \[
533	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
534	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
535	  \]
536	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
537	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
538	
539	  \medskip
540	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
541	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
542	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
543	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
544	
545	  \medskip
546	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
547	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
548	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
549	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
550	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
551	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
552	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
553	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
554	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
555	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
556	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
557	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
558	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
559	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
560	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
561	  an isomorphism.
562	
563	  \medskip
564	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
565	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
566	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
567	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
568	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
569	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
570	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
571	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
572	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
573	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
574	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
575	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
576	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
577	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
578	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
579	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
580	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
581	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
582	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
583	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
584	  (the naturality remark in the proof of
585	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}). This proceeds in three
586	  movements.
587	
588	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
589	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
590	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
591	  \[
592	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
593	      \;\cong\;
594	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
595	  \]
596	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
597	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
598	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
599	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
600	
601	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
602	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism
603	  \(e_\top\) (the global-sections comparison of
604	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}) is precisely the open-naturality square of the
605	  family \(\{e_U\}_U\) (established in the proof of
606	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}), instantiated at the inclusion
607	  \(D(a) \subseteq \top\): the square
608	  \[
609	    \begin{array}{ccc}
610	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, \top\bigr)
611	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_\top\;}
612	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top)\bigr) \\[4pt]
613	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}}\big\downarrow & &
614	        \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}} \\[4pt]
615	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
616	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{D(a)}\;}
617	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr)
618	    \end{array}
619	  \]
620	  commutes, using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
621	  definitional. Because the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) is a natural isomorphism, this single
622	  square is available uniformly in \(a\); there is no per-\(a\) section-level identity
623	  to re-prove by hand. It intertwines the source restriction
624	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) on the pushforward with the (restriction-of-scalars
625	  reading of the) target restriction \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}\) on
626	  \(\widetilde M\).
627	
628	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target restriction
629	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} : \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
630	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) =
631	  M[1/\varphi a]\) as the \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of
632	  \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) over \(R'\); this is exactly
633	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}. Apply the ring-change converse
634	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
635	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
636	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) —
637	  its \(\operatorname{Algebra} R\, R'\) and scalar-tower hypotheses being supplied by
638	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
639	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
640	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
641	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
642	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property
643	  across that square — via the transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized
644	  modules — yields that \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) is the
645	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
646	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)
647	  is performed: the only \(a\)-dependent input is the instantiation of the single
648	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
649	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
650	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
651	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
652	
653	  \medskip
654	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
655	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
656	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
657	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
658	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the naturality
659	  remark in the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the family
660	  \(\{e_U\}_U\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the
661	  structure-sheaf restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this
662	  open-naturality that drives the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the
663	  per-\(a\) localization fact \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level
664	  localization Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with that
665	  naturality square for the inclusion
666	  \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a section-level naturality square
667	  re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\). This is the single remaining
668	  obligation of the construction; the carrier of the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
669	  sections is the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\), against which
670	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} applies directly.
671	
672	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
673	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
674	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
675	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
676	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
677	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
678	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
679	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
680	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
681	
682	  % NOTE: This lemma is the affine case of the general fact that the pushforward of
683	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
684	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
685	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
686	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.
687	  % The in-tree proof remains valid and is not made redundant by a future Mathlib
688	  % bump.
689	  \medskip
690	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
691	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
692	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
693	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
694	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
695	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
696	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
697	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
698	  needed for the affine lane.
699	\end{proof}

701	\begin{remark}
702	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
703	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
704	  \begin{enumerate}
705	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
706	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
707	      \(R\)-module of sections of
708	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
709	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
710	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
711	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
712	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
713	      closed under isomorphism; hence
714	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
715	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
716	      needed for the affine lane.
717	  \end{enumerate}
718	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
719	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
720	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
721	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
722	\end{remark}

906	\begin{lemma}
907	\label{lemma-affine-base-change}
908	Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
909	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
910	Assume $f$ is affine.
911	In this case $f_*\mathcal{F} \cong Rf_*\mathcal{F}$ is
912	a quasi-coherent sheaf, and for every base change diagram
913	(\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
914	we have
915	$$
916	g^*f_*\mathcal{F} = f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}.
917	$$
918	\end{lemma}
919	
920	\begin{proof}
921	The vanishing of higher direct images is
922	Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.
923	The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
924	assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
925	$S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
926	for some $A$-module $M$.
927	We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
928	to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
929	Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
930	$\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
931	to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
932	Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
933	equality
934	$$
935	(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
936	$$
937	as $R'$-modules.
938	\end{proof}

942	special case of cohomology and base change. It follows immediately
943	from the stronger results in

947	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change]
948	\label{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}
949	Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
950	$$
951	\xymatrix{
952	X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
953	S' \ar[r]^g & S
954	}
955	$$
956	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
957	with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
958	Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
959	For any $i \geq 0$
960	\begin{enumerate}
961	\item the base change map of
962	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
963	is an isomorphism
964	$$
965	g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
966	$$
967	\item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
968	$H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
969	\end{enumerate}
970	\end{lemma}
971	
972	\begin{proof}
973	Using Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case} in (1)
974	is allowed since $g'$ is flat by
975	Morphisms, Lemma \ref{morphisms-lemma-base-change-flat}.
976	Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
977	part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
978	and $S'$ are affine. In other words, we have $S = \Spec(A)$
979	and $S' = \Spec(B)$ as in (2).
980	Then since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent
981	(Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}),
982	it is the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_S$-module associated to the
983	$A$-module $H^0(S, R^if_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$
984	(equality by
985	Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}).
986	Similarly, $R^if'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent
987	$\mathcal{O}_{S'}$-module associated to the $B$-module
988	$H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$. Since pullback by $g$ corresponds
989	to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules
990	(Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback})
991	we see that it suffices to prove (2).
992	
993	\medskip\noindent
994	Let $A \to B$ be a flat ring homomorphism.
995	Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme over $A$.
996	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
997	Set $X_B = X \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$ and denote
998	$\mathcal{F}_B$ the pullback of $\mathcal{F}$.
999	We are trying to show that the map
1000	$$
1001	H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
1002	$$
1003	(given by the reference in the statement of the lemma)
1004	is an isomorphism.
1005	
1006	\medskip\noindent
1007	In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
1008	$\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
1009	$$
1010	\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
1011	$$
1012	see
1013	Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
1014	If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
1015	by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
1016	and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
1017	$$
1018	\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
1019	$$
1020	We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
1021	$$
1022	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
1023	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
1024	$$
1025	as follows from
1026	Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
1027	Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology.
1028	
1029	\medskip\noindent
1030	In case $X$ is quasi-separated, choose an affine open covering
1031	$\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$. We will use the
1032	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence
1033	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence}.
1034	The reader who wishes to avoid this spectral sequence
1035	can use Mayer-Vietoris and induction on $t$ as in the proof of
1036	Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}.
1037	The spectral sequence has $E_2$-page
1038	$E_2^{p, q} = \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))$
1039	and converges to $H^{p + q}(X, \mathcal{F})$.
1040	Similarly, we have a spectral sequence with $E_2$-page
1041	$E_2^{p, q} = \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))$
1042	which converges to $H^{p + q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)$.

1044	and separated, the result of the second paragraph of the proof gives

1047	Using that $A \to B$ is flat we conclude that
1048	$H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)$
1049	is an isomorphism for all $i$ and we win.

Let \(f : X \to S\) be an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, with a base-change square as above. The formation of
  the base-change map \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is compatible with restriction
  to affine opens of \(S'\): for each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\), the
  restriction of the base-change map to sections over \(U\) agrees with the
  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
  by the affine-open locality criterion
  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).

  \medskip
  \emph{Intended formalized signature.} With the ambient data
  \(f : X \to S\), \(g : S' \to S\), \(f' : X' \to S'\), \(g' : X' \to X\) and a
  proof \(h\) that the square is cartesian (\(h : \mathrm{IsPullback}\ g'\ f'\ f\ g\),
  whose commutativity \(h.w : g' \circ f = f' \circ g\) feeds the base-change map),
  under the hypotheses \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) and
  \([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\), the lemma is the locality reduction
  \[
    \bigl(\forall\, U : S'.\mathrm{affineOpens},\
      \mathrm{IsIso}\ \bigl((\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\ f\ g\ f'\ g'\ h.w\
        \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{app}\ U\bigr)\bigr)
    \;\Longrightarrow\;
    \mathrm{IsIso}\ \bigl(\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\ f\ g\ f'\ g'\ h.w\
      \mathcal{F}\bigr),
  \]

  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} for the base-change map, with the
  per-affine-open hypothesis being exactly the affine--affine section assertion that
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} supplies. The content
  beyond the bare locality criterion is the identification of the restriction of
  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')
  \subseteq S'\) with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over a

  \([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\) makes \(\mathcal{F}\) restrict to
  \(\widetilde{M}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
  The base-change map \(\alpha = \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\) is the transpose,
  under the \((\text{pullback} \dashv \text{pushforward})\) adjunction for \(g\), of
  the composite
  \[
    f_*\widetilde{M}
    \xrightarrow{\;f_*(\eta')\;} f_*(g')_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}
    = (g' f)_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}
    = (f' g)_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}
    = g_* f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M},
  \]
  where \(\eta'\) is the unit of the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction and the middle
  equalities are the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward and the commutativity of the
  square. We compute \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) on the module of global sections, tracing a
  generator and naming the adjunction unit/counit expanded at each step. Throughout we
  use the two affine dictionaries to read each functor on modules:
  \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-pushforward is restriction of scalars
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}) and \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-pullback is
  extension of scalars (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}). On global sections,
  \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), so \((g')^*\widetilde{M}\) reads as the
  \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).

  \medskip
  \emph{Step 1: the \(((g')^*,(g')_*)\)-unit \(\eta'\).} Under the dictionaries,
  \((g')^* = (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A (-)\) (extension of scalars along
  \(\theta : A \to R' \otimes_R A\)) and \((g')_*\) is restriction of scalars back
  along \(\theta\). The unit \(\eta'_{\widetilde M}\) on global \(A\)-modules is the
  base-change unit
  \[
    M \longrightarrow (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M, \qquad
    m \longmapsto 1 \otimes m = (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m,
  \]
  i.e.\ \(m \mapsto 1 \otimes m\) into the extension of scalars, read back as an
  \(A\)-linear map into the restriction of scalars.

  \medskip
  \emph{Step 2: apply \(f_* = \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\) and read off the
  pseudofunctor identifications.} Applying \(f_*\) (restriction of scalars along
  \(\varphi : R \to A\)) turns \(\eta'\) into the same assignment
  \(m \mapsto 1 \otimes m\) read as an \(R\)-linear map
  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M \to \operatorname{restr}_{R \to R' \otimes_R A}
  \bigl((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\bigr)\); the pseudofunctor equalities
  \((g' f)_* = (f' g)_* = g_* f'_*\) reindex the target as
  \(g_* f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}\), i.e.\ as
  \(\operatorname{restr}_\psi\) of the \(R'\)-module
  \(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M} = \operatorname{restr}_{R' \to R' \otimes_R A}
  \bigl((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\bigr)\). This last \(R'\)-module is exactly the
  target identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\): the global sections of
  \(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}\) are \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) as an
  \(R'\)-module.

  \medskip
  \emph{Step 3: transpose under the \((g^*, g_*)\)-counit/adjunction for \(\psi\).}
  The map \(\alpha\) is the \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-transpose of the morphism
  \(f_*\widetilde{M} \to g_* f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}\) just computed; under the
  dictionaries \(g^* = R' \otimes_R (-)\) (extension along \(\psi\)) and \(g_*\) is
  restriction along \(\psi\). The transpose of an \(R\)-linear map
  \(u : \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M \to \operatorname{restr}_\psi N\) (here
  \(N = (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\)) is the unique \(R'\)-linear map
  \(\widehat{u} : R' \otimes_R M \to N\) with
  \(\widehat{u}(r' \otimes m) = r' \cdot u(m)\); this is precisely the counit of the
  extension/restriction adjunction expanded at \(N\). Here the source
  \(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M})\) reads as \(R' \otimes_R M\) — this is the source
  identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\). Thus
  \[
    \Gamma(\alpha) : R' \otimes_R M \longrightarrow (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M,
    \qquad
    r' \otimes m \longmapsto r' \cdot \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)
      = (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m,
  \]
  using that the \(R'\)-action on \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) scales the
  \(R'\)-factor.

  \medskip
  \emph{Step 4: identify with \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\).} The map
  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\) is exactly the inverse of the
  cancellation isomorphism
  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange} : (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \to R' \otimes_R M\),
  \((r' \otimes a) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\): tracing the generator
  \((r' \otimes a) \otimes m\) the other way, \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\)
  absorbs the inner \(A\)-factor \(a\) into the module via \(a \cdot m\) and leaves the
  \(R'\)-scalar \(r'\), and \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\bigl((r' \otimes 1)
  \otimes m\bigr) = r' \otimes m\). Hence
  \(\Gamma(\alpha) = \operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\), conjugated by
  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) and \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) as in the intended signature.
  As \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is a Mathlib linear equivalence carrying no
  flatness hypothesis, \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an isomorphism, and the affine case is
  closed: the section-level map is computed \emph{directly}, with no separate
  abstract-mate-versus-concrete-map identification at the level of sheaves.
\end{proof}

% with a quick sub-step:
%   (1) AFFINE REDUCTION. S, S', X, X' are arbitrary (only f affine, F quasi-
%       coherent); reducing to S=Spec R, S'=Spec R', X=Spec A, F=M~ needs the
%       naturality / base-change compatibility of pushforwardBaseChangeMap under
%       restriction to affine opens of S', which is itself not packaged in Mathlib.
%   (2) ADJOINT-MATE <-> cancelBaseChange. Even once affine, the abstract adjoint-
%       mate pushforwardBaseChangeMap transported through the four dictionary isos
%       must be shown equal to tilde(TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange)
%       -- a coherence computation unwinding the mate of the ((g')^*,(g')_*)-unit.
% cancelBaseChange itself is present in Mathlib with no flatness; the gap is the
% mate identification, not the closing algebra. See
% informal/affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso.md and the in-file sorry comment.
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:base_change_map_affine_local, lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
  \emph{First reduction (locality).} By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). Fix one such affine
  open. The assertion is then local on \(S\) and on \(S'\), so we may assume all
  schemes in sight are affine: \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
  \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), with \(f\)
  corresponding to a ring map \(R \to A\) and \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) the
  quasi-coherent sheaf associated to an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that
  quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) is used: it is what licenses writing
  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) over the affine open.) Computing the fibre product
  gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), and the pullback
  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to
  the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). In this affine
  situation the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.

  \medskip
  \emph{Reduction to a concrete module map via full-faithfulness of tilde.} The
  functor \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful, with essential
  image precisely the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently, the counit comparison
  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms; by naturality of the counit, a
  square relates \(\alpha\) to \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\alpha)}\), and since the two
  vertical counit maps are isomorphisms,
  \[
    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
  \]
  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map of
  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). This reframing keeps the whole computation at
  the level of modules and morphisms of affine schemes; in particular it requires
  no manipulation of section-level scalar-multiplication or module instances. The
  ring map underlying the pushforward at the top open is the global-sections map
  \(f_{\top}\) of the morphism (which for \(f = \operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is
  conjugate to \(\varphi\) via the naturality of the \(\Gamma\)--\(\operatorname{Spec}\)
  unit), and the preimage of the top open under \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is
  again the top open, so the identification of sections needs no transport. Any
  residual scalar identity in \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) is settled by the
  scalar-tower compatibility of restriction of scalars.

  \medskip
  \emph{Identification of the two sides via the affine dictionaries.} The two
  quasi-coherent sheaves whose comparison \(\alpha\) is the section-level
  base-change map are identified over the affine chart by the two affine
  dictionaries. On the one hand, the \emph{pullback dictionary}
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} identifies the restriction of
  \((g')^*\mathcal{F} = (g')^*(\widetilde{M})\) to the chart with the tilde of the
  base-changed module: writing \(g' = \operatorname{Spec}(\theta)\) for the induced
  ring map \(\theta : A \to R' \otimes_R A\),
  \[
    (g')^*\widetilde{M} \;\cong\; \widetilde{\bigl((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\bigr)},
  \]
  the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\);
  pushing forward along the affine \(f' = \operatorname{Spec}(R \to R' \otimes_R A)\)
  and then reading global sections recovers \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) as an
  \(R'\)-module by restriction of scalars
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}). On the other hand, the
  \emph{pushforward dictionary} Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
  identifies \(f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*(\widetilde{M})\) over \(S\) with
  \(\widetilde{\operatorname{restr}_{R \to A} M}\), and the pullback dictionary
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} then identifies its pullback
  \(g^*(f_*\mathcal{F})\) with \(\widetilde{R' \otimes_R M}\); reading global
  sections gives the \(R'\)-module \(R' \otimes_R M\). Thus, under the dictionaries,
  the source and target of \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) are the two iterated tensor products
  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) and \(R' \otimes_R M\), both viewed as
  \(R'\)-modules.

  \medskip
  \emph{Identification of \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) with the cancellation isomorphism.} The
  base-change map \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is the global-sections incarnation of
  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}),
  which is constructed abstractly as an \emph{adjoint mate}: it is the transpose,
  under the \((\text{pullback} \dashv \text{pushforward})\) adjunctions for \(g\) and
  \(g'\), of the morphism obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The crux of the remaining Lean work is
  to match this abstractly-defined adjoint mate, transported through the two
  dictionaries above, with the \emph{concrete} canonical \(R'\)-linear comparison
  between the two iterated tensor products — that is, with the associativity /
  cancellation isomorphism
  \[
    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M
  \]
  of \(R'\)-modules (Mathlib's
  \texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}). Concretely the map
  sends \((r' \otimes a) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\), using the
  \(A\)-action on \(M\). Once the identification of the abstract adjoint mate with
  this concrete \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is made — the sole nontrivial
  remaining obligation — the affine case closes immediately, because
  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis
  whatsoever; flatness of \(g\) enters only in the global (non-affine) statement
  via the {\v C}ech argument, not here.
\end{proof}

% obligations: lem:base_change_map_affine_local (locality reduction on the affine
% opens of S') and lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange (the section-
% level value of the base-change map is cancelBaseChange). Both proved affine tilde

% feed the second obligation. cancelBaseChange is in Mathlib with no flatness; the
% affine case carries no flatness hypothesis. No Čech infrastructure is used here.
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:base_change_map_affine_local, lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
  The proof is the composite of the two named obligations, taken \emph{directly on
  global sections}.

  \medskip
  \emph{First reduction (locality on \(S'\)).} By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). This is exactly the
  locality reduction Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}: restricting the
  cartesian square to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R') \subseteq S'\) and
  a chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), the
  hypothesis \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to
  \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict
  to \(\widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that quasi-coherence is
  used: it is what licenses writing \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\).) Computing the
  fibre product gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\) and
  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module
  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). The matter is thus reduced to the affine--affine
  model, where the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.

  \medskip
  \emph{Passage to global sections.} The functor
  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful with essential image
  the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently the counit
  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms and, by their naturality,
  \[
    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
  \]
  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map in
  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). The whole computation therefore stays at the level
  of modules.

  \medskip
  \emph{Section-level identification (the second obligation).} By the section-level
  computation Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, the map
  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\), transported through the pullback dictionary
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} (which identifies its source with
  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\)) and the pushforward dictionary
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} (which identifies its target with
  \(R' \otimes_R M\)), \emph{is} the canonical cancellation isomorphism
  \[
    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
  \]
  of \(R'\)-modules (Mathlib's
  \texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}). This is the entire
  content of the affine close, and it is performed \emph{directly on the module of
  global sections} — there is no separate abstract adjoint-mate identification at the
  level of sheaves. Because \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with
  no flatness hypothesis whatsoever, \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an isomorphism, hence so is
  \(\alpha\) on the affine chart, and by the locality reduction so is the global
  base-change map. Flatness of \(g\) plays no role in the affine lemma; it enters only
  in the global statement Theorem~\ref{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}, where it is
  used to commute \(- \otimes_A B\) with the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\).
\end{proof}

\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}

The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.

\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}')\). Since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the comparison map of
  \(B\)-modules
  \[
    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
  \]
  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.

  \medskip
  \emph{The separated case via {\v C}ech complexes.} Suppose first that \(X\) is
  separated over \(A\). Choose a finite affine open cover
  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\); by separatedness all finite
  intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are again affine, so {\v C}ech cohomology
  computes sheaf cohomology:
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\). Base changing
  the cover yields an affine open cover
  \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of the again-separated
  \(X_B\), with the same property
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
  affine case (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) identifies each
  term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the original term tensored over
  \(A\) with \(B\); that is, there is an isomorphism of cochain complexes
  \[
    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;\cong\;
    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B .
  \]
  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, \(- \otimes_A B\) is an exact functor and hence
  commutes with the formation of cohomology of a complex; taking \(H^0\)
  (degree-zero cohomology) gives the desired isomorphism
  \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\).

  \medskip
  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated.
  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\).
  Now the intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are quasi-compact and separated
  rather than affine, but they fall under the separated case already treated, so
  flat base change holds for each of them. One then compares the two
  {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequences with \(E_2\)-pages
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\) and
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))\), converging to
  \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\) and \(H^{p+q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) respectively.
  The separated case provides the term-by-term identification
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B\) on the
  \(E_2\)-pages, and flatness of \(A \to B\) propagates this isomorphism through
  the spectral sequences to the abutment. In particular, in degree \(0\) one
  obtains \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as
  required. (For the \(i = 0\) statement at hand, one may avoid the spectral
  sequence entirely and argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of
  cover members, reducing each step to the separated case.)
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
  sequence.

  \medskip
  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
  \[
    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
  \]
  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.

  \medskip
  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
  \[
    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
    \;=\;
    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
      \;\rightrightarrows\;
      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
    \Bigr),
    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
  \]
  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
  higher cohomology.

  \medskip
  \emph{The separated case.} Suppose first that \(X\) is separated over \(A\). Then
  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
  \[
    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
    \qquad
    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
  \]
  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}, i.e.\ Mathlib's
  \texttt{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}):
  \[
    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
  \]
  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.

  \medskip
  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated. We
  argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of members of a finite affine
  cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\), each step reducing to the
  separated/affine case. For \(t = 1\), \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the
  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),
  \]
  and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness of \(A \to B\) makes
  \(- \otimes_A B\) preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
  \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t - 1\) members), and for
  \(W \cap V\) (separated), it follows for \(X\). In degree \(0\) this gives
  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as required.
\end{proof}
